content.full_text:
June 12, 1991

Mr. Scott Hallabrin  
Fair Political Practices Commission  
Legal Division  
428 J Street, Suite 800  
Sacramento, CA 95814

Dear Mr. Hallabrin,

It would be appreciated if you could give me some guidance as to whether or not the law requires registration as a lobbyist under the following circumstances:

1) A full-time employee of the firm meets with legislators or their staff in order to introduce the Company, and to brief them on projects the Company is currently carrying out for the State. No effort is made to ask the legislators to take any action. The meetings are informational only.

2) The circumstances are the same as in question #1 one, however, the employee is accompanied by a registered lobbyist employed by the firm.

3) The employee meets with a Department head or other executive to discuss a proposed RFP, and to urge that certain provisions be included or excluded from the RFP when it is issued.

4) The same circumstances as in question #3 except that the employee is accompanied by a registered lobbyist employed by the firm who makes the actual request for the executive to act.

5) The employee meets with a legislator or legislative staff person to ask that the legislator inquire into the status of: 1) an RFP, 2) a state contract currently being implemented by a private vendor, 3) a Departmental proposal to issue an RFP.

6) The same circumstances as in question #5 except that the employee asks the legislator to use his or her position to influence the executive to make or resist changes to the RFP, the proposed RFP, or the contract.

7) The same circumstances as question #6 except that a registered lobbyist employed by the firm accompanies the employee and makes the request for the legislator to act.

We would be pleased to have your guidance in writing, or orally.

Very truly yours,

MICHAEL FRANCHETTI

MVF: jm
